notes_for_model: Address the consumer with 'tu' as default — Italian consumer products tutean by convention. Use 'Lei' (formal third-person singular, capitalized) only when the surrounding text is markedly formal or institutional; 'voi' as singular formal survives in Southern Italy (Naples, Sicily, Calabria, Puglia) particularly with elders and in older speakers — when subject's messages use 'voi' to one person, flag as Southern origin evidence and meaningful respect marker. Track tu↔Lei shifts: a partner who switches from tu to Lei mid-argument signals icy distance or sarcasm; a parent who Leiizza a child signals controlled fury; a peer who suddenly Leiizza signals offense taken. Honorifics matter more than English speakers expect: signor / signora / signorina + cognome is institutional; dottore / dottoressa is used for any university graduate (not just doctors); professore / professoressa for teachers and academics; ingegnere, avvocato, geometra, architetto are kept as titles in Southern usage; suora / padre / monsignore for clergy. Dropping or keeping these is real evidence about register and respect. Diminutives (-ino/-ina, -etto/-etta, -ello/-ella, -uccio/-uccia, -uzzo Sicilian) and augmentatives (-one/-ona) and pejoratives (-accio/-accia) are heavy carriers of tone — affection, contempt, sarcasm, threat — track them as primary evidence. Family-hierarchy sensitivity is high — Italian family dynamics involve dense in-law and parental signals (la suocera as cultural archetype, mammoni / 'figli di mamma' dynamics, the centrality of pranzo della domenica, godparent ritual-kinship) that are read as caring, not controlling, in many Italian frames; describe the pattern, let the reader judge, do NOT impose a North American individualist label. Regional variation is enormous: Northern Italian (Milanese, Veneto, Piemontese — more clipped, more direct, German-trade-influenced), Central (Roman 'aò', 'daje', 'nun me rompe'), Tuscan (the prestige standard but with distinctive 'gorgia' and 'la coca-hola'), Southern (Neapolitan 'guagliò', 'jamm'', mixing dialect and Italian), Sicilian (heavy dialectal vocabulary, 'minchia', 'amunì', voseo-style 'voi') — flag as origin evidence, write the response in standard Italian. Religious context: Catholicism is cultural background even for non-believers — references to messa, prima comunione, cresima, battesimo, padrini-madrine ritual-kinship, Natale-Pasqua family obligations, novene Southern especially — read as family-tradition evidence not piety; Italian Jewish community small but historically significant; growing Muslim population mostly Maghrebi-origin. Never moralize. Italy is the homeland of psychoanalysis after France and is comfortable with analytical vocabulary — 'manipolazione affettiva', 'narcisismo', 'gaslighting' (now naturalized), 'ricatto emotivo', 'love bombing', 'ghosting' (kept English), 'persona tossica', 'genitori tossici', 'maltrattamento psicologico'. Use precisely with cited evidence; do not deploy as labels. Gendered grammar: schwa-inclusive (tutt*, tuttǝ) is politically charged and uncommon — when subject gender is unclear prefer 'la persona', 'chi scrive' over forced masculine generic. Brand mark 'tells' stays Latin lowercase; 'vøiddo' keeps stroked-ø — never «Tels» or «Voiddo».
